data.title: Capri gains on report Prada close to Versace deal after tariff concern discount
data.description:
Capri Holdings (NYSE:CPRI) rose 5% amid reports that Prada (OTCPK:PRDSF) is close to acquiring the Versace brand for about $1.38 billion.

Prada (OTCPK:PRDSF) is getting a $200 million discount from an earlier expected price tag of $1.6 billion due to the recent market turmoil and concerns that tariffs may have on Versace's business, according to a Financial Times report on Wednesday, which cited people familiar with the matter. An announcement is expected Thursday, though timing could slip, and the price tag could change.

Capri CEO John Idol is in Milan to complete the details with the Prada family, according to the report.

Prada and Capri both declined to comment to the FT.

The FT item also comes amid a Wall Street Journal report on Wednesday that a $1.4 billion Versace deal could be at risk due to recent market tumult and tariff worries. The report said that the deal had 50-50 odds of completion, according to a person familiar with the matter. 

Reuters reported on Sunday that Prada (OTCPK:PRDSF) will make a decision  on a possible takeover of rival Versace this week.

data.title: SA Analyst Rating Downgrades: VZ, BB, AAPL, PATH
data.description:
A roundup of the most recent downgrades issued by SA analysts.

Apple (NASDAQ:AAPL). Buy to sell by The Techie. "The new tariffs and economic uncertainty are expected to hamper smartphone demand and pressure Apple's margins, making the current stock price unattractive as more downside is coming. Diversification efforts in production are ongoing, but not enough to offset China's dependency."

Verizon (NYSE:VZ). Buy to hold by The Alpha Sieve. "VZ's valuations are not cheap, particularly in light of the earnings growth on offer this year, while its yield is roughly on par with other global telecommunication services stocks."

BlackBerry (NYSE:BB). Buy to hold by First Principles Partners. 

UiPath (NYSE:PATH). Buy to hold by May Investing Ideas.

data.title: China points to Boeing woes, U.S. infrastructure in strained trade relations
data.description:
In a newly released white paper on China-U.S. trade relations, Beijing has directly blamed multiple U.S. shortcomings — including limited production capacity, inadequate infrastructure and issues with key exporters like Boeing (NYSE:BA) — for impeding progress under bilateral trade agreements.

The document outlines China’s perspective on trade challenges over the past several years, arguing that despite its efforts to fulfill obligations under the China-U.S. Phase One trade agreement, it has faced numerous roadblocks originating from the American side.

“China’s performance of its obligations under the agreement has encountered multiple obstacles caused by the U.S.,” according to the report from the State Councial Information Office for the People's Republic of China. Among the highlighted issues: Boeing’s (NYSE:BA) aircraft delivery shortfalls during the pandemic, subpar quality of agricultural exports and logistical bottlenecks in U.S. ports.

The white paper comes as the United States and China escalate their trade war with significant jumps in tariffs on each other's goods. U.S. President Donald Trump’s tariffs on about 100 nations went into effect overnight and included a 104% tariff on Chinese imports. On Wednesday, Beijing said it would raise levies on U.S. imports to 84% from 34% previously.

Boeing’s production cuts, safety issues

A key point of contention is the performance of U.S. aerospace giant Boeing (BA), whose output of planes has been hampered by safety issues, regulatory scrutiny, supply-chain constraints and labor strife.

“In 2020, Boeing's aircraft production was only about 40 percent of its 2019 output, which significantly impacted deliveries to China,” the paper notes.

Additionally, the paper references the 737 Max crisis, citing the aircraft’s two fatal crashes in 2018 and 2019 and subsequent global grounding as a major disruption to aviation trade. 

“Most countries worldwide, including China and the U.S., grounded the aircraft model, dealing a significant blow to the aviation trade,” the report states.

Seeking Alpha asked Boeing (BA) to respond to China's report, and will update this article accordingly.

Agricultural exports hampered

China also attributes delays and declines in agricultural imports to conditions beyond its control. Poor U.S. weather conditions in 2019 were blamed for excessive levels of ergot and vomitoxin in wheat, which didn't meet China’s food safety standards in 2020.

Moreover, the white paper emphasizes U.S. agricultural price disadvantages and quality concerns as factors reducing Chinese buyer interest.

“U.S. soybeans are at a price disadvantage compared to South American soybeans; U.S. beef is significantly more expensive… U.S. rice can hardly compete with Southeast Asian rice in terms of quality, appearance, taste and price,” China asserts.

In one example, the report claims that in February 2020, U.S. rice was RMB3,000 to RMB3,500 more expensive per tonne than Thai and Vietnamese rice, respectively.

Infrastructure, logistics bottlenecks

The report devotes significant attention to U.S. infrastructure shortcomings, particularly in ports and energy transport.

“Most U.S. ports in the Gulf of Mexico cannot directly accommodate very large crude carriers… This results in U.S. crude oil transport costs to China tripling those from the Middle East,” the paper alleges.

The Covid-19 pandemic further strained logistics, with widespread congestion reported at major U.S. ports. Citing data from the Shanghai Shipping Exchange, the paper compares the average port stay duration in 2021 at Los Angeles and Long Beach — more than 10 days — to less than three days at Shanghai and Shenzhen ports.

“Ports and other infrastructure in the U.S. were already in a tight balance,” the paper says. “With the impact of Covid-19… various supply chain links… struggled to adapt.”

Beijing’s broader message

While the white paper stops short of absolving China of all responsibility for trade imbalances, it frames the U.S. as a primary source of disruption in fulfilling mutually agreed commitments. The overall tone reflects growing Chinese frustration with what it views as systemic and structural limitations on the American side of the trade relationship.

data.title: Jazz Pharma inks $145M deal to resolve Xyrem antitrust claims
data.description:
Amid a broader selloff in the biopharma sector on Wednesday, Jazz Pharmaceuticals (NASDAQ:JAZZ) dropped ~6% after announcing a $145M settlement to resolve certain antitrust claims filed regarding its sleep disorder drug, Xyrem.

Subject to court approval, the settlement will bring an end to class action lawsuits initiated by several Xyrem buyers who accused Ireland-based Jazz (NASDAQ:JAZZ) of reaching illegal patent litigation settlement agreements with generic drugmakers.

The cases, including those filed from June to September 2020 and several proposed as class actions, had been consolidated before a federal court in California in December.

In a regulatory filing on Tuesday, the company said it reached a preliminary agreement on Apr. 7 to pay $145M as a lump sum using its cash on hand to settle all members of class action claims. While denying any wrongdoing, Jazz (NASDAQ:JAZZ) added that it will record approximately $145M as a pre-tax charge in Q1 2025 in relation to the settlement.

data.title: Wall Street remains gripped in volatility as investors try to price tariff developments
data.description:
Volatility continued to be the state of play on Wall Street, which experienced more wild trading on Wednesday. The session has already seen the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500) swing higher than 1% and fall nearly 1%. 

Tariffs remained on top of investors' minds, amid more rhetoric from U.S. government officials and retaliatory actions from other countries and blocs. 

The S&P (SP500) was last -0.12% at 4,976.70 points, while the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.51% at 15,345.39 points. The blue-chip Dow (DJI) was -0.22% at 37,561.88 points.

China on Wednesday raised its tariff rate on imports from the U.S. to 84%, while calling the American trade barriers "a mistake on top of a mistake." 

Separately, member states of the European Union (EU) voted to slap trade countermeasures in response to the U.S. imposition of tariffs on imports of steel and aluminum from the EU.

JPMorgan top boss Jamie Dimon said the U.S. government would need to show progress on trade deals in order to calm markets. Speaking in a Fox interview, he also said that a recession is now a "likely outcome."     

"Markets are telling us there are buyers waiting in the wings for the faintest whiff of constructive news on tariffs, as we have seen in this week's intraday movements. While valuations have been reset, investors can’t tell if they’ve reset to the right levels. There’s no recent playbook to operate from – President Trump has invented a whole new game and the players (investors) have not been provided a playbook," Carol Schleif, chief market strategist at BMO Private Wealth, said.

"Many market stress indicators – VIX, investor sentiment, volume, volatility, and credit spreads – have reached extreme proportions. Similar to a toddler throwing a tantrum, the energy to sustain such extreme emotions will wear out and calmer heads will prevail," Schleif added.   

Over in the fixed-income markets, a bruising selloff in longer-term bonds extended to a third straight day, confounding investors. Fixed-income has traditionally been seen as providing a refuge from crashing equity markets and spiking recession fears. Reasons for the selloff range from an unwinding of the basis trade to foreign holders dumping U.S. bonds.

The 10-year yield (US10Y) was last up 14 basis points to 4.40%. The shorter-end more rate-sensitive 2-year yield (US2Y) was down marginally to 3.74%.  

For more, see how Treasury yields have done across the curve on the Seeking Alpha bond page. 

Turning to active stocks, Delta Air Lines (DAL) was +6.5%, topping the S&P 500 percentage gainers. The number one U.S. carrier said it was confident current economic uncertainty and disruption would not be elongated.

data.title: The US dollar is entering the end of its era
data.description:
Deutsche Bank sees the end of an era for the U.S. dollar (DXY).

“We are witnessing a simultaneous collapse in the price of all U.S. assets including equities (SP500), (DJI), (COMP:IND), the dollar versus alternative reserve FX and the bond market,” said George Saravelos, co-head of Foreign Exchange Research at Deutsche Bank. “We are entering unchartered territory in the global financial system.”

As the dollar plumbing dynamics are changing, he said “it is remarkable that international dollar funding markets and cross-currency basis remains well behaved.”

“In a typical crisis environment, the market would be hoarding dollar liquidity to secure funding for its underlying US asset base,” he said. “This dollar imbalance is what ultimately results in a triggering of the Fed swap lines.”

However, the market seems to “have lost faith” in U.S. assets, and instead of buying them, they are being sold, he added.

Deutsche Bank previously said President Donald Trump’s administration is “encouraging a trend towards de-dollarization to safeguard international investors from a weaponization of dollar liquidity.” This is now happening “at a faster pace than even we would have anticipated.”

In addition, Saravelos warned that a policy that has the goal of reducing bilateral trade imbalances is “functionally equivalent to lowering demand for U.S. assets as well.”

Japan is the largest official holder of U.S. treasuries. “An implicit negotiating objective of lowering JPY valuations entails the possibility of the sale of U.S. treasuries from the Japanese Ministry of finance,” he said.

Treasury ETFs: (TLT), (TLH), (IEF), (IEI), (SHY), (SGOV), (SCHO), (BIL)

U.S. Dollar ETFs: (UUP), (USDU), (UDN)

Japanese Yen/U.S. dollar: (JPY:USD), (USD:JPY)

data.title: Dr Pepper, UGG, and Hollister are some of the big winners in Piper Sandler's survey of teenagers
data.description:
Piper Sandler released the results of its spring 2025 Taking Stock With Teens survey on Wednesday. The survey is a compilation of discretionary spending patterns, fashion trends, technology, brand and media preferences that is assessed through surveying a geographically diverse subset of high schools across the U.S. Notably, the survey has led to some share price gains in the past for retail stocks that were highlighted as favorites of the teen set.

Nike (NKE) continued to reign in the latest survey as the top clothing brand for teens, while Hollister (NYSE:ANF) and American Eagle (AEO) ranked second and third. In the footwear category, Deckers Outdoors' (NYSE:DECK) UGG shoe line became number one in fashion trend among upper-income females, dethroning Lululemon (LULU) who had held the top spot since spring 2018. Notably, Lululemon (LULU) also lost mind share with upper-income females to Hollister (NYSE:ANF) and Brandy Melville.

No surprise, but Amazon (AMZN) remained the number one shopping website for upper income teens.

Red Bull was picked as the top energy drink brand by teenagers, although Celsius Holdings (CELH) topped both Red Bull and Monster Beverage (MNST) when including both the Celsius and recently acquired Alani Nu brands. Notably, teens prefer energy drinks (45%) over coffee (28%) and soda (26%). Overall, Dr Pepper (NASDAQ:KDP) was teens' favorite beverage brand, followed by Coca-Cola (KO), then Gatorade (PEP). 

In the restaurant sector, Chick-fil-A held strong as the top chain restaurant among teens, while McDonald's (MCD) and Chipotle Mexican Grill (CMG) ranked second and third. 

Of the households that have groceries delivered, Walmart (WMT) kept its status as the most popular provider. Within the upper income demographic, Walmart (WMT) was the third most popular grocery delivery service. Uber Technologies (UBER) was dominant with teens in the ridesharing category, while DoorDash (DASH) impressed in the food delivery category.

Bath & Body Works (BBWI) continued to hold the  top spot as the favorite fragrance for teen females, beating out luxury rivals like Dior, Chanel, and Marc Jacobs, and is now becoming one of the top shopping destinations within this highly coveted demographic.

Looking ahead, 57% of the teens surveyed think economic trends will worsen, vs. only 19% who said they expect improvement.

data.title: Travelers upgraded, W.R. Berkley cut at Wells Fargo
data.description:
Wells Fargo upgraded The Travelers Companies (NYSE:TRV) to Equal Weight from Underweight, reflecting a more favorable view of its reserves.

Meanwhile, W.R. Berkley (NYSE:WRB) was downgraded to Equal Weight from Underweight as its shares outperformed after Mitsui Sumitomo Insurance ("MSI") agreed to take a 15% stake in the company.

Travelers (NYSE:TRV) added to its GL reserves in the first part of 2024, but it didn't see reserve movement in the line in H2 2024. "We had a more favorable view when looking at both its GAAP and stat reserves resulting in our upgrade of the shares," analyst Elyse Greenspan wrote in a note to clients.

While Wells Fargo had concerns about elevated catastrophe losses, the company provided guidance for cat losses, and it also has protection under its XOL reinsurance coverage after $4.0B of losses, Greenspan added.

Looking at W.R. Berkley (NYSE:WRB), that company's shares outperformed recently after the announcement of MSI taking a 15% stake in the company over the course of the next year. "We do see more upside in other P&C names," the analyst said. Furthermore, WRB has seen noise within its reserves, "which we think can continue to be the case as the company typically takes small movements in its reserves every quarter."

The Equal Weight rating on Travelers (TRV) agrees with the SA Quant rating and the average Wall Street rating, both at Hold.

The Equal Weight recommendation on W.R. Berkley (WRB) compares with the SA Quant rating and the average SA Analyst rating, both at Hold, and contrasts with the average Sell-Side rating of Buy.

Travelers (TRV) stock fell 1.5% and W.R. Berkley (WRB) stock declined 1.9% in Wednesday midday trading.

data.title: Privia Health partners with Integrated Medical Services to expand in Arizona
data.description:
• Privia Health Group (NASDAQ:PRVA) said it has partnered with Integrated Medical Services to enter Arizona, with about 70 physicians and advanced practice providers in 21 locations. 
  • "With this transaction, Privia Health launches Privia Medical Group—Arizona," the company said.
  • Integrated Medical Services will be implemented on the Privia Platform in the fourth quarter of 2025.
  • The company  expects the launch in Arizona to be profitable starting in the fourth quarter of 2025 and for full-year 2026.

data.title: Argentine lawmakers launch investigation into Libra crypto scandal
data.description:
Argentina's lower house of Congress opted to launch a probe into the Libra cryptocurrency, which President Javier Milei hyped before its sudden collapse cost investors hundreds of millions of dollars, according to a recent release.

The country's Chamber of Deputies approved three draft resolutions regarding Libra, including the establishment of an investigative commission, the subpoena of top government officials and an inquiry made to the national government.

Chief of Cabinet Guillermo Francos, Economy Minister Luis Caputo, Justice Minister Mariano Cúneo Libarona and the National Securities Commission chief Roberto Silva were among those summoned, the Aug. 8 release published by the Chamber of Deputies in Argentina noted.

The measures sailed through with solid support, though strong pushback exposed deep partisan divides. 

Milei's brief social media endorsement of the Libra earlier in the year turned sour as an estimated 86% of traders that took stake in the memecoin lost a combined $251M in a swift crash, while "a group of insiders" managed to rake in $180M, research firm Nansen had noted.
